agent1_snippets.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Multiple myeloma (MM) is a malignant disorder of B cells that involves the proliferation of abnormal monoclonal plasma cells in the bone marrow and/or extramedullary sites; it is the second most common hematological malignancy associated with significant morbidity and mortality by causing end-organ damage. Over the last few decades, MM survival rates have been prolonged due to recent advancements by the advent of novel therapies. Targeted agents and combination therapies have resulted in better outcomes, but most MM patients eventually relapse, including those who achieved remission.

agent1_snippets.title: Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: For high-risk transplant-ineligible patients, there are several options. One approach would be to employ the VRd regimen for 8 to 12 cycles followed by maintenance bortezomib-based therapy. Another option could include daratumumab, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone (DRd) continued until disease progression based on the MAIA trial showing improved progression-free survival and overall survival compared to Lenalidomide and Dexamethasone. [18] This may be particularly helpful in patients ineligible for bortezomib-based therapy. For standard risk transplant-ineligible patients, options include VRd for 8 to 12 cycles followed by lenalidomide maintenance, or DRd continued until disease progression. [19] [20]

agent1_snippets.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Patients who fail to achieve at least minimal response on initial therapy and progress while on treatment are termed "primary refractory." According to the criteria developed by the International Myeloma Working Group (IMWG), relapsed refractory MM (RRMM) is defined as a progressive disease, poor response despite treatment, progression within 60 days of the most recent treatment in a patient who had achieved remission, the absence of at least minimal response (MR), or primary refractory MM. agent1_snippets.content: In cases of triple-class refractory disease, where patients have failed multiple lines of treatment, including PIs, IMiDs, and mAbs against CD38, recently approved therapies are selinexor and dexamethasone; VdT-PACE (bortezomib, dexamethasone, thalidomide, cisplatin, doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide, and etoposide); melflufen-dexamethasone and CAR-T-cells, idecabtagene, and belantamab mafoditin. agent1_snippets.content: In 2022, the FDA approved teclistamab, the first bispecific T-cell engager antibody against CD38 expressed on the myeloma cells, for patients with relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma who have failed 4 prior lines of therapy. Belantamab mafodotin is anti-BCMA humanized immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibody conjugated with monomethyl auristatin. The drug received accelerated approval in the US for patients who had progressed on at least 4 lines of therapy. It is being withdrawn from the market based on the results of randomized phase 3 trials (DREAMM-3), as it did not show clinical benefit compared to pomalidomide and dexamethasone.

[15] As a result, the following were added to the CRAB criteria as diagnostic alternatives to myeloma defining events: Bone marrow plasma cells (BMPCs) equal to 60% Involved/uninvolved serum free light chain ratio greater or equal to 100 Abnormal MRI with more than one focal lesion, with each lesion greater than 5 mm that were often missed on previous skeletal surveys

agent1_snippets.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Multiple myeloma is an incurable hematological malignancy, but recent advances in the treatment have improved the survival outcomes in patients. Despite treatment, multiple myeloma patients are at high risk of relapse and need prompt treatment to reduce the morbidity and mortality associated with the disease and its complications. Patients who fail to achieve at least minimal response on initial therapy and progress while on treatment are termed "primary refractory." Multiple myeloma is considered "double refractory" if the disease has progressed during or after treatment with a protease inhibitor and an immunomodulatory agent. If the patient is also resistant to adding monoclonal antibodies, it is considered a "triple-class" refractory disease, which confers a poor prognosis. This activity outlines the evaluation and management of relapsed and refractory multiple myeloma and highlights the role of the healthcare teams in improving care for patients with this condition.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 43
agent2_snippets.content: Multiple myeloma, classified as a plasma‑cell disorder (اضطراب خلايا البلازما), can be managed with a variety of therapeutic agents. The alkylating agent melfalan is reported to have activity against multiple myeloma, as are its related formulations melphalan flufenamide and melphalan hydrochloride. Immunomodulatory therapy with interferon alfa‑2a may also be employed in the treatment of this disease. The proteasome inhibitor ixazomib is another option that may treat multiple myeloma, providing a targeted approach to malignant plasma cells. Additionally, the bisphosphonate kyselina zoledronová (zoledronic acid) is used to address bone disease associated with multiple myeloma and may contribute to overall disease control. Together, these agents represent a multimodal strategy for treating multiple myeloma, reflecting its classification as a plasma‑cell disorder.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993
agent2_snippets.content: Zoledronic acid (kyselina zoledronová) is recognized as a therapeutic agent that may be used in the treatment of multiple myeloma, a malignant plasma‑cell disorder. As a potent bisphosphonate, zoledronic acid helps to manage the skeletal complications commonly associated with multiple myeloma by inhibiting osteoclast‑mediated bone resorption, thereby reducing the risk of pathological fractures and hypercalcemia. Incorporating zoledronic acid into the therapeutic regimen for multiple myeloma can therefore contribute to improved bone health and overall disease management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1602
agent2_snippets.content: Crizanlizumab is classified within the therapeutic class of Immunological Agents [TC] and is designated as an Immunomodulator (disposition). It is a specific type of Selective adhesion molecule inhibitor (product), reflecting its mechanism of action that targets adhesion molecules to modulate immune cell interactions. Additionally, crizanlizumab is recognized as an imunosupresiva, indicating its broader role as an immunosuppressive agent. Together, these classifications emphasize crizanlizumab’s function as an immunological drug that modulates immune responses through selective inhibition of adhesion molecules, positioning it within both the immunomodulator and immunosuppressive categories of therapy.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
agent2_snippets.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2327
agent2_snippets.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)) includes a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions. Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is classified as an autoimmune disease, as is non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) and acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب). Inflammatory joint disease (التهاب المفاصل) and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are also considered members of this autoimmune group. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying autoimmune mechanism guides clinicians toward appropriate immunomodulatory treatments and vigilant monitoring for systemic involvement.
